interactions.summary: Jiang attacks institutional science as selectively materialist: enormous funding goes to particle physics, outer space, AI, and colliders while consciousness, imagination, spiritual experience, love, telepathy, animal intelligence, and ancient sites remain neglected. He then says specialization, bureaucracy, and credentialism slow discovery, while secret-society priorities can explain why certain paradigms become famous quickly.

claims.claim: Jiang argues that science directs money toward particle physics, outer space, AI, and colliders because these projects serve a materialist paradigm rather than the most humanly important questions.
He says science neglects consciousness, imagination, intuition, spirits, religious truth, kindness, happiness, love, telepathy, animal and plant intelligence, and ancient architecture.
He identifies specialization, funding bureaucracy, and credentialism as reasons modern science has not produced major discoveries and as mechanisms that prevent truth discovery.
He uses Kuhn, Einstein, Darwin, and Disraeli to argue that paradigm change is not merely natural but can be promoted when it advances the eschatology of secret societies.

signature_moments.moment: The forbidden science list becomes a map of Jiang's missing world: consciousness, imagination, love, telepathy, plants, ancient sites.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It shows what he thinks modern materialism excludes, which is as important as what it studies.

signature_moments.moment: Science is said to be bad at creating new things but good at faking things, including a possible alien invasion.
signature_moments.source_phrase: not very good at creating new things, but it's very good at faking things
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is one of the lecture's sharper provocations about institutional science as spectacle.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The rule that only people with sanctioned degrees and institutional positions may speak about science.
ASR/transcript phrase around Disraeli and Jewish-control narratives; Jiang uses it as an example of conspiracy discourse serving the return-to-Israel plan.

chronology_notes.note: This packet gives the science/worldview critique that links materialism to the eschatological project.

uncertainty_notes.note: The collider and “Big Brother” passage mixes Jacob Frank interpretation with speculative interdimensional claims; extraction preserves it as Jiang's model.
The phrase “Kuhn-speak” is likely ASR or local shorthand and should be checked against transcript audio before public quotation.
